source.title: Engineering of Methylation State Specific 3xMBT Domain Using ELISA Screening

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The wild-type 3xMBT domain binds both mono- and di-methylated lysine peptides with similar affinity, limiting its utility as a methylation-state-specific probe. The goal is to engineer 3xMBT variants that exclusively recognize di-methylated lysine, enabling discrimination between methylation states. A high-throughput ELISA screening platform was developed to detect 3xMBT binding to methylated peptides/proteins, and used to screen a double site saturation mutagenesis library targeting residues 361 and 411, which are known to be important for binding. The process yielded mutants with enhanced specificity for di-methylated lysine, providing new insights into MBT-methyllysine interactions.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: The 3xMBT domain was used to develop an ELISA for high-throughput detection of methylated lysines. The assay detects methylated peptides, proteins, and PKMT activity. It was optimized for crude E. coli lysates to screen 3xMBT mutant libraries. A double site saturation library at residues 361 and 411 was generated and screened, yielding variants that exclusively recognize di-methylated peptides. This approach can be applied to engineer domains for other PTMs.
process_signals: WT 3xMBT binds mono- and di-methylated lysines, but the goal is to engineer variants specific for di-methylation.
ELISA was developed and optimized for high-throughput screening in crude lysates.
Double site saturation mutagenesis at positions 361 and 411 was performed.
Screening identified variants with exclusive di-methyl specificity.
methods: High-throughput screening in E. coli lysates

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Lysine methylation exists in three states (me1, me2, me3) and is important in disease. Existing tools (tritium-labeled SAM, pan-methyl antibodies) cannot distinguish methylation states well. Methyl-lysine-binding domains like 3xMBT have been used as affinity reagents, but WT 3xMBT binds both mono- and di-methylated lysines. The goal is to engineer 3xMBT to be specific for di-methylated lysine using a high-throughput ELISA.
process_signals: Need for tools that distinguish methylation states.
WT 3xMBT binds mono- and di-methylated lysines, limiting its use as a state-specific probe.
Hypothesis: mutations in the binding pocket can alter specificity.
failures_or_limits: Existing methods (tritium-labeled SAM, pan-methyl antibodies) cannot efficiently discriminate methylation states.

section_gists.heading: Materials and Methods
section_gists.gist: This section is empty in the provided text.

section_gists.heading: 3XMBT double site saturation mutagenesis
section_gists.gist: Double site saturation mutagenesis was performed on positions 361 and 411 of 3xMBT. A library was constructed by PCR with NNS primers, assembled, and cloned into pGEX-6p1. The library was transformed into E. coli DH-5α, validated by sequencing, and plasmid library purified. An in-vitro methylation assay using SETD7 is also described.
process_signals: Positions 361 and 411 were chosen because they were previously shown to be important for binding mono- and di-methylated substrates.
Library was generated using NNS codons for full saturation.
Library size not explicitly stated, but colonies were harvested for plasmid purification.
methods: Double site saturation mutagenesis via PCR with NNS primers

section_gists.heading: High-throughput ELISA detection of peptides and full-length proteins lysine methylation
section_gists.gist: A 96-well ELISA was developed: streptavidin-coated plates capture biotinylated peptides; purified MBT protein or E. coli lysate with overexpressed MBT is added; detection via anti-FLAG and HRP-conjugated secondary antibody with TMB substrate. For full-length proteins, in-vitro methylation is performed before adding to GST-coated plates, using MBP-MBT to avoid GST dimerization.
process_signals: ELISA was optimized for high-throughput screening in crude E. coli lysates.
Assay can detect methylated peptides, proteins, and PKMT activity.
Use of MBP-MBT for full-length protein detection to avoid GST dimerization issues.
methods: In-vitro methylation with SETD7 for full-length proteins
MBP-MBT fusion to avoid GST dimerization

section_gists.heading: Mutant protein library expression
section_gists.gist: The mutant plasmid library was transformed into E. coli BL21 (DE3). Individual colonies were picked into 96-deep well plates, grown, induced with IPTG, lysed with BugBuster, and cleared lysates were used in the ELISA. Clones were catalogued and frozen.
process_signals: Screening was performed on individual clones in 96-well format.
Lysates were directly used in ELISA, enabling high-throughput screening.

section_gists.heading: Structure modeling
section_gists.gist: Structural modeling used PDB coordinates from electron density server. The human SCML2 MBT repeats in complex with H2A peptide (monomethyl lysine, PDB 4EDU) was modified or mutated in Coot. The L3MBTL1 3-MBT repeats structure (dimethyl lysine analog, PDB 1OYX) was overlapped to orient dimethyl lysine. Figures prepared with PyMOL.
process_signals: Modeling was used to interpret mutant specificity changes.
Structures of related MBT domains with mono- and di-methylated peptides were used as templates.
methods: Coot for model building and mutation

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is empty in the provided text.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The high-throughput ELISA was developed to detect methylated lysines and PKMT activity. It was optimized for screening in crude E. coli lysates. The assay was used to engineer MBT variants specific for di-methylated lysine, providing new insights into MBT-methyllysine interactions. The engineered domain complements existing tools and can be combined with mass spectrometry to study di-methyllysine abundance. Future engineering could yield domains specific for mono- or tri-methylation. The approach can be applied to other PTMs.
process_signals: The ELISA was successfully used to screen mutant libraries and identify di-methyl-specific variants.
The engineered MBT variant can be used with other methods to study di-methylation.
Limitations: context dependence, off-target binding not explicitly discussed, but future engineering is envisioned.
failures_or_limits: The engineered domain may have context-dependent binding or off-target effects, but these are not detailed.
Further engineering is needed for mono- and tri-methyl specific domains.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: WT 3xMBT lacks methylation-state specificity, so engineering is required to create a di-methyl-specific binder.
Mutations at positions 361 and 411 can alter the binding pocket to favor di-methylated lysine over mono-methylated.
A high-throughput ELISA screening platform can efficiently identify desired mutants from a saturation mutagenesis library.
Structural modeling can rationalize the altered specificity of the engineered variants.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the exact binding affinities and specificities of the isolated mutants (e.g., NNS3A1, NNS3F5, NNS4A10)?
What was the library size and screening coverage?
Were there any intermediate failures or negative results during library construction or screening?
How was the ELISA signal normalized for protein expression levels?
What were the structural details of the mutant binding pockets that conferred di-methyl specificity?
Were there any off-target binding or context-dependent effects observed with the engineered domains?
How did the mutants perform in detecting methylation on full-length proteins and in complex lysates?

events.summary: WT 3xMBT binds both mono- and di-methylated lysines, limiting its use as a state-specific probe.
An ELISA was developed using purified Flag-GST-3XMBT as a methyl lysine binder to detect methylated peptides/proteins.
The ELISA was tested with WT 3xMBT and D355N inactive mutant on RelA and H3K36 peptides with different methylation states.
The inactive D355N mutant showed low binding to all peptides, confirming binding specificity.
Double site saturation mutagenesis was performed on positions 361 and 411 of 3xMBT to engineer specificity.
The ELISA was optimized for high-throughput screening in crude E. coli lysates.
Mutant library was expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) and individual clones were screened in 96-well format.
Structural modeling was used to interpret mutant specificity changes using PDB coordinates.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers problem statement, ELISA design, initial validation, library construction, screening setup, and structural modeling approach.

events.summary: ELISA validated for detecting mono- and di-methylated peptides and PKMT activity using 3xMBT domain.
ELISA adapted to detect methylation on full-length proteins using MBP-MBT fusion to avoid GST dimerization.
ELISA shown to work in crude E. coli lysates, enabling high-throughput screening of MBT mutant libraries.
Double site saturation mutagenesis library generated at residues 361 and 411 of 3xMBT to engineer di-methyl specificity.
Screening of ~360 mutants identified clones with >3.5-fold increased di-methyl/mono-methyl binding signal over WT.
Clones NNS3A1, NNS3F5, NNS4A10 specifically bind only di-methylated RelA peptide, confirmed with lysates and purified proteins.
methods_or_tools: full spectrum of methylated RelA peptides (me0, me1, me2, me3)

events.summary: Sequence analysis revealed T411L mutation in all di-methyl-specific clones, suggesting L411 confers specificity.
Structural modeling hypothesizes T411L increases hydrophobicity, excluding water and disfavoring mono-methyl binding while stabilizing di-methyl.

events.summary: Hypothesis that T411L mutation alone may increase selectivity toward di-methyl lysines.
Binding specificity of recombinant T411L 3XMBT mutant compared to WT and NNS3F5.
Structural model supports decreased affinity to mono-methylated lysine while maintaining affinity to di-methylated lysine.
ELISA optimized for high-throughput screening of MBT mutant libraries in crude E. coli lysates.
Engineered MBT variants highly specific to di-methylated lysine were obtained.
Further engineering needed for domains specific to mono- or tri-methylated proteins.

evidence_bank.quote: the WT 3XMBT domain bound mono- and di-methylated peptides, obtaining high ELISA binding signal relative to the signal for un-methylated or tri-methylated peptides
To develop an ELISA for the detection of methylated peptides/proteins we utilized purified Flag-GST-3XMBT as a methyl lysine binder (Fig 1A).
We first tested the ability of the assay to detect differences in the methylation state of a RelA peptide (300–320) and H3K36 peptide–both un-methylated, mono-, di- and tri-methylated [17,21].
we found that the WT 3XMBT domain bound mono- and di-methylated peptides, obtaining high ELISA binding signal relative to the signal for un-methylated or tri-methylated peptides.
the inactive 3XMBT D355N mutant exhibited low binding to all peptides

To perform double site saturation mutagenesis on positions 361 and 411 on 3XL3MBTL1, a short fragment of the MBT gene containing these two amino acid positions was amplified by PCR using a mix of primers: fr-MBTL361-NNS, and rev-MBTT411-NNS
We also optimized the assay to detect 3XMBT binding in crude E. coli lysates which facilitated the high throughput screening of 3XMBT mutant libraries.
Cleared E. coli lysates containing overexpressed MBT protein were subsequently loaded onto the high throughput ELISA plate for lysine methylation detection.
The human SCML2 MBT repeats in a complex with histone H2A peptide (monomethyl lysine, pdbID-4EDU)[18] structure was modified according to its electron density or mutated using Coot [19].

The structure of the Lethal(3)malignant brain tumor-like protein 1 3-MBT repeats (dimethyl lysine analog, PDBID-1OYX) [20] was overlapped on 4EDU using the least square fit in Coot to allow the correct orientation of the dimethyl lysine.
These experiments validated the ELISA method using the 3XMBT domain to detect mono and di-methylated peptides and demonstrated that it can also be used to monitor PKMT activity on specific peptides.
To avoid GST dimerization between the recombinant RelA and the 3XMBT, we utilized a MBP (maltose binding protein) MBT fusion protein.

We observed higher ELISA binding signal for the WT RelA relative to the signal detected for the K37R and K314R/K315R mutants, consistent with the decrease in available methylation sites in these mutant proteins.
These experiments demonstrate the utility of the assay to detect 3XMBT binding to methylated peptides in crude E. coli lysates and pave the way for the screening of 3XMBT mutant libraries.
Thus, we generated a 3XMBT mutant library by double site saturation mutagenesis of residues 361 and 411.
Each of the 400 3XMBT mutants was overexpressed in E. coli and individually tested for binding to mono and di-methylated RelA peptides
Mutants exhibiting more than a 3.5-fold increase in binding signal for the di-methylated peptide over the mono-methylated peptide relative to the WT protein were selected for further analysis.

Using both cell lysates and purified recombinant proteins (Fig 4C and S1 Fig, respectively), we found that all clones can specifically bind only the di-methylated RelA peptide, demonstrating our ability to engineer a 3XMBT that is highly specific for one methylation state.

Strikingly, sequence analysis of these mutants revealed that, while position 361 could be substituted with different amino acids, position 411 was mutated to leucine in all variants, suggesting that L411 confers high binding specificity for di-methylated lysine

Analysis of the binding site of the T411L mutant reveals that the mutation to leucine can significantly increase the hydrophobicity of the binding site, leading to an exclusion of the water molecule and consequently to disfavored binding to mono-methylated lysine
Based on the structural model we hypothesized that the leucine substitution mutant at T411 may be sufficient to increase the selectivity toward di-methyl lysines.
Based on the structural model we hypothesized that the leucine substitution mutant at T411 may be sufficient to increase the selectivity toward di-methyl lysines.
the binding specificity to different peptides of recombinant T411L 3XMBT mutant was compared to the WT and the NNS3F5 recombinant 3XMBT proteins (Fig 5D and S3 Fig).
our data supports a model by which these structural changes can lead to a decreased affinity to mono-methylated lysine while maintaining affinity to the di-methylated lysine.
We have optimized the assay to detect MBT binding in crude E. coli lysates to enable the high throughput screening of MBT mutant libraries.
we have utilized this assay to engineer MBT variants that are highly specific to di-methylated lysine

We envision that further engineering of methylated lysine binding domains using the ELISA approach can lead to a repertoire of domains capable of specific recognition of mono-methylated, di-methylated or tri-methylated proteins in the cell.
the K37R, K314R/K315R and K37R/K314R/K315R mutants to in-vitro methylation by SETD7 and utilized the ELISA method to detect RelA methylation.

To understand the structural basis for the novel specificity of the engineered MBT mutants and rationalize how the T411L mutation can lead to the exclusion of mono-methylated lysine from the 3XMBT binding site, we have analyzed the crystal structure of the WT 3XMBT bound to mono-methylated lysine and di-methylated lysine analog ligands (Fig 5A–5C).
pGEX-6p1-MBT-T411L mutant was generated by site directed mutagenesis using primers listed in S1 Table as described in Levy et al [17].

event_graph.paper_main_problem: The wild-type 3xMBT domain binds both mono- and di-methylated lysine peptides with similar affinity, limiting its utility as a methylation-state-specific probe. The goal is to engineer 3xMBT variants that exclusively recognize di-methylated lysine.

canonical_events.summary: WT 3xMBT binds both mono- and di-methylated lysines, limiting its use as a state-specific probe.
An ELISA was developed using purified Flag-GST-3XMBT as a methyl lysine binder to detect methylated peptides/proteins.
ELISA validated for detecting mono- and di-methylated peptides and PKMT activity using 3xMBT domain.
The inactive D355N mutant showed low binding to all peptides, confirming binding specificity.
ELISA adapted to detect methylation on full-length proteins using MBP-MBT fusion to avoid GST dimerization.
ELISA optimized for high-throughput screening in crude E. coli lysates.
Double site saturation mutagenesis library generated at residues 361 and 411 of 3xMBT to engineer di-methyl specificity.
Mutant library expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) and ~360 individual clones screened in 96-well format using ELISA.
Clones NNS3A1, NNS3F5, NNS4A10 specifically bind only di-methylated RelA peptide, confirmed with lysates and purified proteins.
Sequence analysis revealed T411L mutation in all di-methyl-specific clones, suggesting L411 confers specificity.
Hypothesis that T411L mutation alone may increase selectivity toward di-methyl lysines.
Binding specificity of recombinant T411L 3XMBT mutant compared to WT and NNS3F5, confirming T411L alone confers di-methyl specificity.
Structural modeling supports that T411L increases hydrophobicity, excluding water and disfavoring mono-methyl binding while maintaining di-methyl affinity.
Further engineering needed for domains specific to mono- or tri-methylated proteins.

edges.reason: The problem of WT 3xMBT lacking specificity motivates the development of an ELISA to detect methylation states.
The designed ELISA is validated for detecting methylated peptides and PKMT activity.
The D355N inactive mutant confirms that the ELISA signal is specific to methyl-lysine binding.
The ELISA is adapted for full-length proteins by using MBP-MBT to avoid GST dimerization.
The ELISA is optimized for high-throughput screening in crude E. coli lysates.
The lack of specificity drives the design of a double site saturation mutagenesis library at key residues.
The optimized ELISA in lysates enables high-throughput screening of the mutant library.
The mutant library is expressed and screened using the ELISA.
Screening identifies clones with exclusive di-methyl specificity, confirmed with purified proteins.
Sequence analysis of the specific clones reveals the T411L mutation.
The prevalence of T411L leads to the hypothesis that this single mutation confers specificity.
The hypothesis is tested by comparing recombinant T411L to WT and NNS3F5.
Structural modeling rationalizes how T411L alters the binding pocket to favor di-methyl over mono-methyl.
The success in engineering di-methyl specificity highlights the need for further engineering for other methylation states.

must_cover_points: ELISA development and validation for methylated peptides/proteins.
Optimization of ELISA for high-throughput screening in crude lysates.
Double site saturation mutagenesis at residues 361 and 411.
Screening of ~360 mutants and identification of di-methyl-specific clones.
Confirmation that T411L mutation alone confers di-methyl specificity.
Structural modeling explaining the specificity switch.
